

**Nutri Vision**

AI-Powered Indian Food Nutrition Estimator

────────────────────────────────

    Milestone 3 report \- Model Architecture, end-to-end pipeline  
GROUP 2

 	9.1 Detection Metrics (mAP)  
 	9.2 Segmentation Metrics (IoU, Dice)  
 	9.3 Weight Estimation Metrics (MAE, RMSE)

# **1\. Introduction**

Nutri Vision is an AI-powered mobile application that allows users to photograph a plate of food, including complex Indian multi-dish meals, and instantly receive a detailed nutritional breakdown per food item. The system handles multiple foods on a single plate, estimates portion weights using pixel-area analysis, and retrieves accurate nutritional data through an RAG-powered LLM pipeline.

Milestone 3 covers  preprocessing of all datasets, description of model architecture, and a final end-to-end pipeline across 5 core system components:

* **Food Detection:** YOLO fine-tuned to detect multiple Indian food items on a plate simultaneously using bounding box predictions.  
* **Segmentation:** pixel-level masks for each detected food item are generated, enabling precise area estimation for downstream weight prediction, especially in overlapping multi-dish scenarios.  
* **Weight Estimation:** A custom-collected dataset and MLP regression model that maps food pixel area to weight in grams.  
* **Nutrition Lookup:** Indian Food Nutritional Values dataset (Kaggle 2025\) for per-100g macro and micronutrient retrieval.  
* **Personalised Suggestions:** MedGemma \+ Gemini API with ICMR RDA guidelines integrated via Pinecone RAG for health-aware dietary recommendations.

# **2\. Dataset Organization**

**2.1 Dataset Overview**

Three different food image datasets were used and merged for training the object detection model. These datasets contain diverse food categories, varying image conditions, and annotation styles.

* [South Indian Food Detection Dataset](https://universe.roboflow.com/south-indian-food-detection-and-classification/food-detection-nlusn) (31 classes, specific to South Indian cuisine)  
* [IndianFoodNet-30](https://universe.roboflow.com/indianfoodnet/indianfoodnet) (30 classes)  
* [Indian Food Yolov5](https://universe.roboflow.com/smart-india-hackathon/indian-food-yolov5) (region-specific dishes preprocessed in YOLOv5 format, 81 classes)

The entire dataset includes **120 distinct food categories**, 39185 images, and no duplicate class names, enabling the model to recognize a wide variety of dishes. These class labels are defined in the configuration file and are used during both training and inference. The dataset was extracted and verified after preprocessing. The distribution of images and corresponding annotation files across different splits is shown below:

| Split | Number of Images | Number of Labels | Annotated Images | Empty Labels |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Train | 34,905 | 34,905 | 34,879 | 26 |
| Validation | 2,405 | 2,405 | 2,403 | 2 |
| Test | 1,875 | 1,875 | 1,873 | 2 |

Each image has a corresponding label file in YOLO format, ensuring a one-to-one mapping between images and annotations. The majority of images are properly annotated. A very small number of images contain empty label files.

**2.2 Directory structure**

The dataset follows the YOLO directory format:

![][image1]

# **3\. Dataset Preprocessing**

**3.1 Preprocessing**

Since the project combines multiple datasets, inconsistencies were observed in class naming conventions across datasets. These include:

* Different spellings (e.g., *briyani* vs *biryani*)  
* Different naming formats (e.g., *Idli* vs. *Idli*)  
* Regional variations (e.g., *satham* vs *white\_rice*)  
* Case and formatting differences (e.g., *PalakPaneer* vs *palak\_paneer*)

To address this, a canonical mapping strategy (Mapping multiple class variations to a single standardized label) was implemented to standardize all class labels, and the labels were normalized to ensure semantic consistency. After applying label normalization across all three datasets, the class labels were merged to form a unified and consistent label space after removing duplicates.

To ensure the correctness and quality of the dataset, first, a subset of images from the training split was visualized along with their corresponding YOLO bounding box annotations.

A filtering approach was applied to select images containing a specific class (*sambar\_satham*) for focused inspection. Random samples from this subset were then displayed with bounding boxes and class labels overlaid on the images.

**3.1.1 Class Index Alignment**

After creating a unified set of class labels, it is necessary to align the class indices from each dataset to the new unified class space. Each dataset originally uses its own class indexing (e.g., class 0 in DS1 may represent a different food item than class 0 in DS2). Therefore, a remapping strategy was implemented where, after normalization and sorting were done, new indices were assigned.

![][image2]

Later, a validation step was performed to ensure that all annotation files contain valid class IDs that correctly map to the unified class space. To create a unified training dataset, the three source datasets (DS1, DS2, DS3) were then merged into a single dataset after applying label normalization and class ID remapping.

**3.1.2 Visualization check**

To ensure the correctness and quality of the dataset, a subset of images from the training split was visualized along with their corresponding YOLO bounding box annotations.

* Bounding boxes accurately capture the food regions in most images  
* Class labels correctly correspond to the visual content  
* The dataset includes variations in:  
  * Lighting conditions  
  * Plate types and orientation  
  * Background complexity

![][image3]

The dataset was analyzed to understand the distribution of annotations across all 120 food classes. The analysis includes:

* Total number of annotations per class  
* Number of unique images per class

The dataset contains a total of **56,830 annotations across 120 classes.** The dataset shows significant variation in class distribution. Some classes have high representation, for example:

* *gulab\_jamun*: 1795 annotations  
* *idli*: 1760 annotations  
* *dosa*: 1426 annotations  
* *white\_rice*: 1397 annotations

Some classes have very few samples, for example:

* *chicken\_tikka*: 35 annotations   
* *churma*: 44 annotations 

![][image4]

This shows the high class imbalance the dataset contains. The dataset contains classes with widely varying numbers of annotations. Extremely low-sample classes can lead to poor model learning. Therefore, a threshold-based filtering approach was adopted to improve model performance. A threshold criterion of \<50 annotations was implemented, and classes like chicken\_tikka and churma were removed. Moderate sample classes (50-15- annotations) were retained but subjected to monitoring. Classes with greater than 150 annotations were immediately kept for training. Now the dataset is left with 118 classes.

**3.1.3 Bounding box analysis**

To better understand the spatial characteristics of objects in the dataset, an analysis of bounding box dimensions and distribution was performed. The following properties were analyzed: Bounding box width, Bounding box height, Bounding box area, Number of annotations per image.

![][image5]

* Tiny boxes (\< 1% area): 737 (1.3%)  
* Large boxes (\> 50% area): 24,016 (42.3%)  
* Median bounding box area: 0.4023

The dominance of large bounding boxes indicates that food items occupy a significant portion of the image, making object detection effective but necessitating segmentation for precise portion estimation. Considered filtering 737 tiny bounding boxes as well.

**3.1.4 image quality analysis**

The dataset was analyzed to understand the distribution of image resolutions and ensure compatibility with the model input requirements.

![][image6]

The dataset’s dominant resolution is 640 × 640 → 35,331 images (\~90%), which is the standard YOLO input size. The rest are 416 × 416 → 3,854 images (\~10%) are also valid.The dataset contains very limited variation in resolution, and the majority were already aligned with YOLO requirements

**3.1.5 Class-wise data indexing**

To enable efficient retrieval of samples for each class, a class-wise indexing mechanism was implemented. This avoids repeated file scanning and improves data access performance during analysis and visualization. 

* A dictionary was created to map (image filename (stem) → full image path)  
* Each label file is read once  
* Unique class IDs present in the image are identified  
* A mapping is created

**3.2  Cleaning**

**3.2.1 Removing classes**

To improve dataset quality and reduce class imbalance, a subset of underrepresented and less relevant classes was removed from the dataset. The selected classes included:

*chicken\_tikka, churma, gavvalu, chak\_hao\_kheer, lyangcha, doodhpak, qubani\_ka\_meetha, shankarpali, pootharekulu.*

Instead of removing entire images, a fine-grained filtering approach was adopted. All annotations corresponding to the selected classes were removed from label files, while retaining images that contained other valid classes. Now we have 111 classes in the dataset.

After removing annotations, some images were left without any associated objects. These images and their corresponding empty label files (395) were automatically identified and removed to maintain dataset consistency.

**3.2.2 Removing small bounding boxes**

To improve annotation quality, extremely small bounding boxes (covering less than 0.1% of the image area) were identified and removed. These boxes are often indicative of noise or labeling errors and can negatively impact model performance. (Removed 124 tiny boxes from 113 files)

**3.2.3 YOLO ready dataset after resizing and class balancing.**

After removing selected classes, class indices were remapped to ensure a continuous and consistent labeling scheme. This step is essential for compatibility with YOLO-based models, which require class IDs to be sequential, starting from zero.

* Old classes: 120  
* New classes: 111

Images with a resolution of 416×416 were resized to 640×640 to align with the input size commonly used in YOLO-based models.

![][image7]

* Single class image → 37579 images  
* Multi-class image → 1207 images  
  * 2 classes: 1151 images  
  * 3 classes: 49 images  
  * 4 classes:  7 images

To address class imbalance, a maximum cap of 500 images per class was applied to the training set. Excess samples from overrepresented classes were removed. Priority was given to removing single-class images to minimize information loss. For multi-class images, removal was avoided if it negatively impacted underrepresented classes. Total images deleted from training: 7589\.

![][image8]

To further address class imbalance, underrepresented classes were augmented using Albumentations. Transformations such as horizontal flipping, brightness/contrast adjustment, color variation, rotation, and noise injection were applied. Bounding box-aware augmentation was employed to ensure spatial consistency between images and annotations. This process increased the number of training samples for minority classes, improving model robustness and reducing bias.

![][image9]

**Final check:**

![][image10]![][image11]

# **4\. Model Architecture**

## **4.1 Data Flow Representation in Architecture**

The Nutri Vision system follows a multi-stage pipeline to generate nutritional insights from food images. A raw input image (640×640) is first processed using a YOLOv12s model for object detection, producing class labels and bounding boxes. Pixel area is then calculated for each detected item and used by an MLP regressor to estimate weight in grams. Finally, these weights are mapped to a nutrition database to obtain values such as calories, protein, carbohydrates, and fats, with a future extension incorporating a RAG-based system for enhanced recommendations.

The pipeline can be summarized as:  
 **![][image12]**

## **4.2 Object Detection Model (YOLO)**

We use YOLOv12s for object detection due to its real-time performance and high accuracy. The model is fine-tuned on a dataset of Indian food images with 120 classes.

Key features:

* Detects multiple food items in a single image

* Provides bounding boxes and class labels

* Handles variations in lighting, occlusion, and backgrounds

YOLO is chosen because of:

* Fast inference speed (suitable for mobile apps)

* High precision and recall for dense object detection tasks

## **4.3 Segmentation Model – SAM-2**

Since the trained model is a detection model (bounding boxes only), pixel-precise segmentation is achieved using SAM-2 by Meta AI. SAM-2 (Segment Anything Model 2\) is a foundation model developed by Meta AI that generates high-quality pixel-level segmentation masks for objects using prompts such as bounding boxes or points. It works in a zero-shot manner, meaning it can segment unseen objects without requiring task-specific training.

Approach:

•⁠  ⁠YOLO bounding box is passed as a prompt to SAM-2

•⁠  ⁠SAM-2 generates a pixel-level binary mask within the bbox

•⁠  ⁠No training or fine-tuning required (zero-shot)

Benefits:

•⁠  ⁠Works on any food image without food-specific training

•⁠  ⁠Pixel-precise mask improves weight estimation accuracy

  over bbox-area approximation

•⁠  ⁠Handles complex food shapes and textures

## **4.4 Weight Estimation Module**

The weight estimation module converts pixel area into real-world weight (grams).

Method:

* Extract pixel area from segmentation masks

* Use a regression model (MLP) which has been trained on labeled data that maps pixel area to actual weight.

Formula (conceptually): ***Weight \= f(Pixel Area)***, where the model learns the function from data.

Features:

* Learns mapping between visual size and actual quantity

* Works across different food types with learned patterns

## **4.5 Nutrition Estimation Module**

The nutrition estimation module computes nutritional values based on predicted food type and estimated weight.

Steps:

1. Retrieve per-100g nutritional values from dataset

2. Scale values using estimated weight

3. Aggregate results for all food items

Additionally:

* Uses RAG-based system (Pinecone \+ LLM) for enhanced recommendations

* Supports personalized dietary suggestions

# **5\. Input and Output Specifications**

## **5.1 Input Format**

• Image type     : JPEG / PNG / WebP                              
• Image size      : Any (resized to 640×640 internally)            
• Channels        : RGB (3-channel)                                
• Normalization : Pixel values scaled to \[0, 1\]   

## **5.2 Intermediate Representations**

• YOLO output    : \[class\_id, x, y, w, h, confidence\]            
• Mask output      : Binary mask per detected food item             
• Features           : \[pixel\_ratio, pixel\_food, pixel\_plate, class\_encoded\]  

## **5.3 Output Format**

• Food class        : String label (e.g., "biryani", "idli")         
• Weight              : Float in grams (e.g., 245.3g)                 
• Calories            : kcal (scaled from per-100g CSV)                
• Protein              : grams                                          
• Carbohydrates  : grams                                          
• Fat                    : grams    

# **6\. Justification of Model Choice**

## **6.1 Advantages of Proposed Architecture**

* **End-to-End Automation:** The system provides a fully automated pipeline, converting raw food images directly into detailed nutritional information without requiring manual input.  
* **Multi-Food Detection Capability:** It can accurately detect and process multiple food items present in a single image, making it suitable for complex meals, especially Indian thali-style dishes.  
* **Modular Architecture:** Each component (detection, segmentation, weight estimation, and nutrition lookup) is designed independently, allowing easy upgrades, maintenance, and experimentation without affecting the entire system.  
* **Real-Time Performance:** The use of YOLO-based object detection ensures fast inference, enabling near real-time predictions suitable for mobile and web applications.  
* **Scalability:** The system can be easily extended to include additional food categories by retraining the detection model and updating the nutrition database.  
* **Improved Accuracy through Multi-Stage Processing:** Combining detection, segmentation, and regression improves overall prediction accuracy compared to single-stage approaches.

## **6.2 Limitations**

While the proposed architecture demonstrates a working end-to-end pipeline for Indian food nutrition estimation, several limitations exist in the current implementation that present opportunities for future improvement.

**6.2.1 Detection Limitations**

* **Small dataset per class** — 111 classes but uneven distribution, some classes have very few training images leading to poor detection for rare items  
* **Detection not segmentation** — current model outputs bounding boxes only, not pixel-precise masks (SAM-2 was added separately to compensate)  
* **Similar looking foods** — visually similar Indian foods like dal/sambar, various biryanis, different chutneys cause frequent misclassification  
* **Single food assumption** — when multiple foods overlap on a plate, YOLO may merge or miss detections  
* **Lighting and angle sensitivity** — models trained on standard images may fail on dark, blurry or unusual angle photos


  
**6.2.2 Segmentation Limitations**

* **Depends on YOLO box quality** — if YOLO gives a wrong or loose bounding box, SAM-2 mask will also be inaccurate  
* **No food-specific training** — SAM-2 is general purpose, it segments the most prominent object in the box which may include plate/background  
* **Cannot distinguish overlapping foods** — if two foods touch or overlap, SAM-2 may segment both as one region


  
**6.2.3 Weight Estimation Limitations**

* **Very small training set** — only 88 images, 63 usable after filtering, which is insufficient for a reliable regression model  
* **Pixel ratio alone is weak** — same food class at different distances from camera gives different pixel areas for same actual weight  
* **No depth information** — a tall glass of lassi and a flat spread of rice may have similar pixel areas but very different weights  
* **Camera distance not normalized** — images taken from different heights give inconsistent pixel-to-gram ratios  
* **Class imbalance** — some classes appear only once in training data (e.g., ghevar, kulfi) making weight estimation unreliable for those  
  


**6.2.4 Pipeline Limitations**

* **No plate detection** — we use full image area as plate proxy instead of detecting the actual plate boundary, reducing pixel ratio accuracy  
* **Sequential error propagation** — wrong YOLO detection → wrong SAM-2 mask → wrong pixel features → wrong MLP prediction, errors compound at each stage  
* **Static nutrition lookup** — nutrition values are fixed per 100g from CSV and do not account for cooking method, oil content or regional variations  
* **No real-time optimization** — current pipeline runs YOLO \+ SAM-2 \+ MLP sequentially which is slow for a mobile app use case

# **7\. End-to-End Pipeline Implementation**

## **7.1 Subset Dataset Execution**

To verify the working of the complete Nutri Vision system, the pipeline was first executed on a subset of the prepared test dataset. This subset-based execution was performed to ensure that the trained detection model can correctly process food images and generate structured outputs that can later be passed to the segmentation, weight estimation, and nutrition estimation modules.

In the current milestone, the focus of the end-to-end execution is on the **object detection stage**, because detection is the first and most important component in the pipeline. If food items are not detected correctly, then the downstream stages such as segmentation, weight estimation, and nutrition lookup will also be affected.

The subset execution followed the same flow as the proposed final system:

**Input image → preprocessing → YOLO detection → predicted food classes \+ bounding boxes \+ confidence scores**

During execution:

* Test images were loaded from the unified dataset

* Images were resized to the model input size

* The trained YOLO model was used for inference

* For each image, the model generated:

  * predicted class label

  * bounding box coordinates

  * confidence score

* The outputs were then visualized for verification

This step confirmed that the model can process images from the merged multi-source dataset and generate valid predictions in the unified class space.

The subset execution also helped verify that:

* Class labels are correctly mapped to the standardized class names

* Bounding boxes are properly aligned with the food objects

* Multiple food items in a single image can be detected

* The outputs are in a suitable format for later modules

At this stage, the subset execution demonstrates that the current implementation is able to successfully perform **image-to-detection inference**, which is the first working stage of the Nutri Vision pipeline.

## **7.2 Pipeline Integration Verification**

After validating the detection model on a subset of the dataset, pipeline integration verification was performed to ensure that the outputs from one module can be correctly used by the next module in the proposed architecture.

Since Nutri Vision follows a modular pipeline, it is important to verify that all components are connected in a consistent manner. The current milestone focuses mainly on confirming the compatibility of the detection outputs with the later stages of the system.

The following integration checks were verified:

### **7.2.1. Input Compatibility**

The system accepts food images in standard formats such as **JPEG, PNG, and WebP**.  
 Images of different resolutions are successfully loaded and resized before inference.

### **7.2.2. Detection Output Verification**

The YOLO detector returns outputs in the required structured format:

* class ID

* class label

* bounding box coordinates

* confidence score

These outputs are consistent with the unified class mapping created during preprocessing.

### **7.2.3. Class Mapping Consistency**

Since multiple datasets were merged, class normalization and remapping were critical. During integration verification, it was confirmed that:

* Predicted class IDs correspond to the unified class index

* Displayed class labels match the actual food item names

* The model outputs remain consistent across images from different source datasets

### **7.2.4. Visualization Verification**

The predicted outputs were successfully visualized on sample test images using:

* bounding box overlays

* class labels

* confidence scores

This confirms that the inference results are interpretable and suitable for manual inspection.

### **7.2.5. Downstream Module Readiness**

Although the current milestone primarily demonstrates the detection stage, the outputs are structured in a way that they can be directly passed to the next modules:

* **Segmentation module:** can use the detected bounding boxes as region prompts

* **Weight estimation module:** can use the detected food region area as an input feature

* **Nutrition estimation module:** can map the predicted food class to nutrition values in the food database

Therefore, the pipeline integration verification confirms that the current system is functionally ready for full end-to-end extension in future milestones.

# **8\. End-to-End Pipeline Implementation**

## **8.1 Example Predictions**

To analyze the practical performance of the trained detection model, sample predictions were generated on images from the test split. These examples show how the model behaves on real food images and help in understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the current implementation.

Two model variants were compared:

* **Demo Model (v3 \- 15 epochs, 416 px)**

* **Main Model (v1 \- 7 epochs, 640 px)**

The sample prediction results show that both models are able to detect multiple Indian food items from the test images, but their behavior is different in terms of confidence, number of detections, and stability.

In the sample outputs, the models were able to detect food classes such as:

* **ven\_pongal**

* **idli**

* **green\_chutney**

* **paneer\_masala**

* **soan\_papdi**

* **nandu\_masala**

* **parupu\_vada**

The **Demo Model** produced more detections in several cases. It showed stronger ability to detect multiple food regions, especially in images containing repeated food items such as sweets. However, it also produced some overlapping or repeated bounding boxes for the same food object.

The **Main Model** produced comparatively fewer detections and in some cases gave cleaner predictions, but it also missed certain food items. For example, in one sample image, the food item detected by the Demo Model was not detected by the Main Model.

From these examples, the following observations can be made:

* Common and visually distinctive food items are detected more reliably

* Dense or repeated food items can lead to multiple overlapping detections

* Visually similar food categories may cause confusion

* The model is able to detect multiple items in a single image, which is important for plate-level analysis

* Detection performance varies depending on image quality, food appearance, and class representation in training data

Overall, the example predictions confirm that the current model is capable of generating meaningful food detections and can serve as the foundation for the downstream nutrition estimation pipeline.

***Figure 8.1:** Sample test detections produced by the Demo Model (v3 \- 15 epochs, 416 px).*

![][image13]

***Figure 8.2:** Sample test detections produced by the Main Model (v1 \- 7 epochs, 640 px).*

![][image14]

## **8.2 Visualization of Outputs**

To further analyze the detection performance, visual summaries were created to compare the performance of the two trained models. These visualizations provide both qualitative and class-wise insights into how the models perform across different food categories.

The first type of output visualization is the annotated test image output, where the predicted bounding boxes, class labels, and confidence scores are shown directly on the images. These visualizations help in understanding whether the model is detecting the correct food region and whether the predicted class is semantically correct.

The second type of visualization is a **Top 20 class-wise AP50 comparison** for both models. This helps in identifying the food categories on which the models perform best.

For the **Demo Model**, the highest AP50 scores were observed for classes such as:

* **kali**

* **appam**

* **beetroot\_poriyal**

* **sambar**

* **boiled\_egg**

* **uzhuntha\_vadai**

* **ven\_pongal**

For the **Main Model**, strong performance was observed for classes such as:

* **beetroot\_poriyal**

* **ven\_pongal**

* **dharwad\_pedha**

* **kali**

* **appam**

* **paal\_kolukattai**

* **boiled\_egg**

These visualizations show that both models perform well on certain visually distinctive food categories. In general, food items with unique shape, texture, or presentation style achieve higher AP50 values. On the other hand, visually similar foods or underrepresented classes are more difficult to detect accurately.

The output visualizations also support the observation that the Demo Model performs better overall on the current dataset. It achieves higher class-wise AP50 values for many categories and shows stronger detection behavior on sample images.

Thus, the visual analysis complements the quantitative metrics and provides a clearer understanding of the strengths and limitations of the current detection model.

***Figure 8.3:** Top 20 class-wise AP50 comparison for the Demo Model and Main Model.*

![][image15]

# **9\. Loss Functions and Evaluation Metrics**

## **9.1 Detection Metrics (mAP)**

To quantitatively evaluate the performance of the object detection model, standard object detection metrics were used on both the validation set and the test set. These metrics provide a reliable measure of how accurately the model detects and localizes food items.

The following metrics were used:

* **mAP@50**: Mean Average Precision at IoU threshold 0.50

* **mAP@50-95**: Mean Average Precision averaged over IoU thresholds from 0.50 to 0.95

* **Precision**: Fraction of predicted detections that are correct

* **Recall**: Fraction of ground-truth objects that are successfully detected

The performance of the two trained model variants was compared:

* **Demo Model (v3 \- 15 epochs, 416 px)**

* **Main Model (v1 \- 7 epochs, 640 px)**

The evaluation results show that the Demo Model performs better than the Main Model on both the validation set and the test set.

### **Validation Set Metrics**

| Metric | Demo Model (v3 \- 15ep 416px) | Main Model (v1 \- 7ep 640px) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| mAP@50 | 55.1% | 32.6% |
| mAP@50-95 | 44.3% | 24.0% |
| Precision | 57.7% | 35.8% |
| Recall | 50.4% | 28.3% |

### **Test Set Metrics**

| Metric | Demo Model (v3 \- 15ep 416px) | Main Model (v1 \- 7ep 640px) |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| mAP@50 | 60.2% | 36.8% |
| mAP@50-95 | 48.5% | 27.1% |
| Precision | 59.8% | 41.3% |
| Recall | 55.3% | 30.5% |

From these results, it is clear that the **Demo Model outperforms the Main Model across all evaluation metrics**.

On the validation set, the Demo Model achieves:

* **22.5% higher mAP@50**

* **20.3% higher mAP@50-95**

* **21.9% higher precision**

* **22.1% higher recall**

On the test set, the Demo Model achieves:

* **23.4% higher mAP@50**

* **21.4% higher mAP@50-95**

* **18.5% higher precision**

* **24.8% higher recall**

These results suggest that, in the current training setup, the number of training epochs had a stronger impact on model performance than the increase in input resolution. Although the Main Model used a larger image size (640 px), it was trained for fewer epochs and therefore did not converge as effectively as the Demo Model.

Based on the current results, the **Demo Model is the better-performing detection model** and is more suitable for downstream integration in the present milestone.

***Figure 9.1:*** *Comparison of validation and test set detection metrics for the Demo Model and Main Model.*

![][image16]

## **9.2 Segmentation Metrics (IoU, Dice)**

Since accurate food portion estimation depends on correct food region extraction, segmentation quality is an important part of the proposed Nutri Vision pipeline. The segmentation module is intended to refine the detected food region and obtain a more precise mask for each detected item.

The following metrics are used to evaluate segmentation performance:

* **IoU (Intersection over Union)**  
   Measures the overlap between the predicted segmentation mask and the ground-truth mask.  
      		![][image17]​  
* **Dice Coefficient**  
   Measures the similarity between the predicted mask and the ground-truth mask.  
  		![][image18]

These metrics are important because better segmentation directly improves the accuracy of pixel-area extraction, which is later used for weight estimation.

In the current milestone, segmentation is included in the proposed architecture, but full quantitative evaluation using a dedicated mask-annotated benchmark set has not yet been completed. At present, the project mainly focuses on validating the detection stage and establishing the correct pipeline flow.

The current segmentation stage is planned as a box-guided refinement step, where the detected bounding boxes can be used as input prompts for a segmentation model. However, due to the absence of a sufficiently large food-specific mask-labeled dataset, detailed numerical IoU and Dice results are not reported in this milestone.

In future work, segmentation performance will be evaluated by:

* Comparing predicted masks with ground-truth masks

* Computing mean IoU across food classes

* Computing mean Dice coefficient across food classes

* Analyzing difficult cases such as overlapping foods and low-contrast boundaries

Thus, IoU and Dice remain the correct evaluation metrics for the segmentation module, and they will be used in the next stage of development when full segmentation benchmarking is performed.

IoU \= |Prediction ∩ GroundTruth| / |Prediction ∪ GroundTruth|

## **9.3 Weight Estimation Metrics (MAE, RMSE)**

The weight estimation module is responsible for converting visual food region information into an estimated food weight in grams. This is an important stage of the Nutri Vision system because nutritional values are calculated by scaling per-100g nutrition values using the predicted food weight.

Since weight estimation is a regression problem, the following metrics are used to evaluate the model:

* **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)**  
   Measures the average absolute difference between the predicted weight and the actual weight.

  ![][image19]

* **RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)**  
   Measures the square root of the average squared difference between the predicted weight and the actual weight.  
  			![][image20]

These metrics are appropriate because:

* **MAE** directly indicates the average prediction error in grams

* **RMSE** penalizes larger errors more strongly and helps identify unstable predictions

In the current milestone, the weight estimation module has been designed as part of the overall architecture, but full quantitative benchmarking is still limited because the available weight-labeled dataset is very small.

As discussed in the limitations section:

* The original dataset contains **88 images**

* After filtering, only **63 usable samples** remained

* Some food classes have very few examples

* Pixel area alone is not sufficient to fully capture real-world food volume

Therefore, the current milestone mainly establishes the feature design and pipeline compatibility for weight estimation, while detailed regression evaluation will be performed in future work.

In the next stage, the weight estimation module will be evaluated using:

* Train/validation/test split on the weight-labeled dataset

* MAE and RMSE on unseen samples

* Comparison of baseline and improved feature sets

* Class-wise error analysis where possible

Potential features for future improvement include:

* food pixel area

* plate-relative pixel ratio

* class encoding

* mask shape descriptors

* camera distance normalization

* depth-aware features

Thus, MAE and RMSE are the correct evaluation metrics for the weight estimation module, and final numerical values will be reported after the weight-labeled dataset is expanded and the regression model is fully trained.

#
